subject: Ventral Hernias
title: Ventral Hernias: The 80% Rule
hook: One ventral hernia fact shows up on almost every exam. Most students miss the rest.

points.heading: Incisional hernias are 80%
points.body: Incisional hernias are the most common ventral hernia, around 80% of cases. They follow laparotomy and, less often, laparoscopic port sites. Wound infection is the single most important cause.

points.heading: Epigastric vs paraumbilical
points.body: Epigastric hernias push through the linea alba between xiphoid and umbilicus, often as protrusions of extraperitoneal fat. Paraumbilical hernias go through the linea alba beside the umbilicus and have a narrow neck prone to strangulation in adults.

points.heading: Richter's hernia is the silent one
points.body: In a Richter's hernia only the antimesenteric wall of the bowel is trapped. It can strangulate and perforate without ever causing complete obstruction, which is exactly why it gets tested.

points.heading: Spigelian: the lateral landmark
points.body: A Spigelian hernia sits along the linea semilunaris, with most below the umbilicus, where the defect lies under an intact external oblique. The landmark name is usually the answer.

caption: Ventral hernias are pure exam gold: the 80% incisional rule, the narrow-neck paraumbilical trap, and the silent Richter's strangulation all turn up again and again. Master the classification and the landmarks and these become free marks. Recall Engine turns any lecture into SBAs, flashcards and high-yield notes so you can drill these exact facts until they stick. Start free, link in bio.
